result:
I have been thinking about the small, stubborn life of a sentence. Not the grand, cathedral sentence that opens *A Tale of Two Cities*, but the everyday sentence that trudges out of bed at five-thirty to let the dog out, finds the coffee can empty, and still shows up for work. The sentence that has a slight headache from yesterday’s argument and carries its comma like a cracked lunchbox. Most people never notice it; they want the fireworks, the semicolon that vaults across a chasm, the colon that promises revelation. I used to want that, too. I wanted sentences that wore capes and landed on rooftops. Now I want the one that remembers to buy milk on the way home.

There is a kind of holiness in the unlovely sentence, the way there is holiness in the grocery list written on the back of an envelope that once held an electric bill. I keep a folder of these sentences, culled from student papers, from margin notes I scribble while waiting on hold, from the bottom of e-mails whose main purpose was to say “attached please find.” One reads: “The garden is still there but the beans gave up.” Another: “I can’t tell if the noise is the fan or my heart.” They are not trying to be literature; they are trying to survive literature the way a beetle survives a shoe. They interest me more than the polished stones in award-winning essays because they still have dirt on their wings. They testify.

I once believed that the job of writing was to ascend: climb the ladder of abstraction until the air got thin and the view turned theological. I wrote sentences like “History is the long ache of the unmet stranger.” Sounds good in a workshop, but it will not keep you company on the night your mother forgets your name. Now I believe the job is to descend, to go down the fire escape into the alley where the grease dumpster warms a family of cats. Down where the pronouns smell of sweat and the verbs have nicotine on their breath. The alley is full of sentences that have been mugged, stripped of their adjectives, left bleeding but still breathing. If you kneel, they will whisper their actual names.

We are living in the age of the announced self. Every thought must be branded, every tremor of the soul hashtagged and launched like a raft that wants to be an ark. Meanwhile the real self—the one that snores, the one that rehearsed an apology in the shower and still didn’t make it—hides in the footnotes, ashamed of its small GDP. I have started hosting clandestine services for that self. Ten minutes at a time, I shut the Wi-Fi router in a drawer and let the room go dark. No audience, no metrics, no possibility of “going viral” unless you count the leftover Spanish flu in the hutch that once belonged to my grandmother. In that hush I copy out somebody else’s plain sentence by hand. “The light switch in the hall sticks when summer comes.” I write it ten times, eleven. I watch my pen turn the t’s into small crosses, like a roadside shrine for a pilgrim who never arrived. By the seventh copy the sentence is no longer “practice”; it is roommate, breathing on the other side of the mattress. It will never trend, but it will walk the dog with me at dawn.

I have begun to suspect that the internet is a giant iron that flattens every bump of specificity. We trade in currencies—trauma, joy, outrage—whose exchange rates are posted hourly. The hotter the coin, the faster it melts the details. Yesterday I saw a photo of a wildfire captioned “Mood.” A forest become mood. Somewhere in the pixel smoke an actual fox is running with her tongue out, trying to save her single kit, but the kit is not shareable content. I click like, I scroll, I forget. The forgetting feels like lint in the lungs. So I return to the stubborn sentence, the one that refuses to be flattened because it is already 2-D and proud of it, the way a shadow is proud of the curb that gives it an edge. “The fox stops, looks back, then disappears into what used to be a ridge.” Try to make that go viral; it would rather go fungal, creeping quietly through the mulch of offline minds.

There is, of course, a politics to the small. Whole economies depend on you feeling too little to matter. If they can keep you convinced that your grocery list is not an epic, you will keep buying epic-sized solutions. But the grocery list is an epic: apples because the old ones went soft, cat food for the beast who shares your pillow though no contract was ever signed, bleach because the world is busy staining everything ochre. Each bullet point a stanza in the long poem of maintenance. Maintenance, friends, is the most revolutionary act left to us. It refuses the apocalypse its dramatic finale; it insists on tomorrow’s dishes. The poem that ends with a sink full of suds is the poem that outlives empires, because suds return irrespective of who is emperor.

Sometimes I entertain the fantasy of founding a Museum of Plain Sentences. Admission would be free, but you would have to surrender your phone at the door. Inside, dim as a confessional, you could linger in front of “The bus was late again” written in the author’s actual bus-late handwriting. Security guards would be trained to hiss if they caught you trying to photograph. The exit would deposit you in an alley—always an alley—where a woman in a yellow coat is clipping laundry to a line that sags between two fire escapes. You would smell onions frying in oil that has seen better decades. No gift shop, no postcard. Just the sentence you left behind breathing more easily now that you have gone.

I worry that I am romanticizing the ordinary, the way certain poets wax rhapsodic over laundromats while never actually sitting on the plastic bench long enough to watch a stranger’s thong whirl against the porthole glass. So I keep appointments with banality the way other people keep therapy. I ride the city bus at noon when the scent blend is part vinyl, part human liver working overtime. I listen for sentences spoken into phones: “He says the rash doesn’t look like shingles but what else climbs a body like that?” I write it down, not to steal someone’s sorrow but to prove to myself that the language still has pores. If I ever find myself writing about “the people” in capital letters, I will sentence myself to a week of only quoting overheard rash updates.

The truth is that grandeur needs grout. Without the sentence that crawls on its belly checking for leaks, the cathedral of Big Ideas sinks into the swamp. We are living in a swamp moment; you can smell the methane of demolished meaning. The only responsible thing is to lay one small sentence after another, like tiles in a mosaic that will never be finished but at least gives the barefoot something to step on. I laid one this morning: “The coffee maker clicked, the same way my father’s jaw clicked when he heard bad news.” It will not get me a keynote, but it bought me another hour of inhabiting my own skin. Somewhere in the circuitry of the machine, a tiny piece of copper remembers the click and carries it forward into someone’s dawn. That is enough. That is everything.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
